reasoning: Applied the error taxonomy (grammar, vocabulary, pragmatic categories) with severity calibrated to A1 CEFR level. Used Ukrainian-English interlanguage patterns to identify L1 transfer errors - particularly article omission (Ukrainian has no articles), preposition confusion ("o" -> "on"), and third person -s omission (Ukrainian verbs do not inflect for person in the same way). Severity is calibrated against A1 expectations: article errors are moderate (expected at this level but must be addressed), while third person -s omission is major (it is the core grammar target of this lesson and recurs persistently).

errors.original: I am working on new project.
errors.corrected: I am working on a new project.
errors.explanation: Missing indefinite article "a" before "new project". At A1, articles are being introduced and are expected to be inconsistent, but this is a high-frequency pattern that recurs throughout the lesson.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian has no article system. Nouns appear without determiners, so Ukrainian speakers systematically omit English articles.

errors.corrected: It's an application. A mobile application.
errors.explanation: Missing subject "it" and missing article. Ukrainian is a pro-drop language that allows subject omission, especially with copular constructions.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian allows null subjects (pro-drop). "Це додаток" does not require an explicit subject pronoun.

errors.corrected: the client wants many things
errors.explanation: Missing third person -s on "want" and missing article "the". This is the single most recurring error pattern in the lesson.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian verbs do not add a suffix for third person singular present tense in the same way. The conjugation system is different, so the English -s/-es ending has no direct equivalent.

errors.original: I must to say time correct.
errors.corrected: I must say the time correctly.
errors.explanation: Added "to" after "must". Also missing article before "time" and adverb form "correctly".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian "мушу" (must) is followed by an infinitive form, and learners transfer the "to" from "have to" pattern. Conflation of "must" and "have to" syntax.

errors.original: Today I wake up on seven o'clock.
errors.corrected: Today I woke up at seven o'clock.
errors.explanation: Used present simple "wake" instead of past simple "woke" for a completed past action. Also preposition error (see #6).
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: partial - Ukrainian aspect system differs from English tense system. The present tense can be used for recent past events in casual Ukrainian speech.

errors.corrected: at seven o'clock
errors.explanation: Used "on" instead of "at" for clock time. This error recurs at 07:17.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian "о дев'ятій" uses the preposition "о" which phonetically resembles "on". Dmytro explicitly identified this transfer himself at 07:38.

errors.corrected: Yesterday I woke up late.
errors.explanation: Repeated past tense error - used present "wake" instead of past "woke" with the time marker "yesterday".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: partial - same as #5

errors.original: My wife wake up at six because she go to... she go to school early.
errors.corrected: My wife wakes up at six because she goes to school early.
errors.explanation: Multiple third person -s omissions in one utterance: "wake" -> "wakes", "go" -> "goes" (twice).
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - same mechanism as #3

errors.original: I start work on nine o'clock.
errors.corrected: I start work at nine o'clock.
errors.explanation: Repeated preposition error "on" for time. Same L1 transfer as #6.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - same Ukrainian "о" -> "on" transfer

errors.corrected: the project has a deadline
errors.explanation: Missing third person -s ("have" -> "has"), missing articles "the" and "a".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - article omission + verb agreement

errors.original: in office... I bring food from home because my wife cook very good.
errors.corrected: in the office... I bring food from home because my wife cooks very well.
errors.explanation: Missing article "the", third person -s on "cook", and adverb "well" instead of adjective "good".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - article omission (Ukrainian), verb agreement (Ukrainian), adverb/adjective conflation (Ukrainian "добре" covers both)

errors.corrected: my wife cooks very well
errors.explanation: Used adjective "good" instead of adverb "well" to modify the verb "cooks".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian "добре" functions as both adjective and adverb

errors.corrected: She makes amazing borshch.
errors.explanation: Third person -s omission on "make".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - same pattern as #3, #8

errors.original: My alarm go off at quarter to seven.
errors.corrected: My alarm goes off at quarter to seven.
errors.explanation: Third person -s omission on "go" (subject is "alarm" = it).
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - same pattern

errors.corrected: I wash my face and brush my teeth.
errors.explanation: Missing possessive determiner "my" before body parts. English requires possessive with body parts where Ukrainian uses reflexive constructions.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian uses reflexive constructions with body parts rather than possessive determiners

errors.corrected: I go by car. / I drive to work.
errors.explanation: Wrong preposition for mode of transport: "on" instead of "by".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian "на машині" uses the preposition "на" which corresponds to "on" in many contexts, but English uses "by" for mode of transport

errors.corrected: She goes by marshrutka.
errors.explanation: Third person -s omission. Same recurring pattern.

errors.corrected: I watch YouTube in the evening.
errors.explanation: Missing article "the" in the fixed phrase "in the evening".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - article omission

errors.corrected: I rest a little bit.
errors.explanation: Missing article "a" in the quantifier phrase "a little bit".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - article omission

errors.original: I am sometimes meeting with friends.
errors.corrected: I sometimes meet friends.
errors.explanation: Used present continuous for a habitual action. Present simple is required for routines and habits.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: partial - Ukrainian imperfective aspect can map to both English present simple and present continuous, causing confusion

errors.original: I am working in office all day and then I am sitting at home.
errors.corrected: I work in the office all day and then I sit at home.
errors.explanation: Repeated present continuous for habitual actions. Should be present simple.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: partial - same as #20

errors.corrected: I know, it's bad.
errors.explanation: Missing subject "it" in copular construction.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian pro-drop allows subject omission

errors.corrected: I always want to start...
errors.explanation: Placed "always" at sentence beginning. English requires "always/never" before the main verb, not sentence-initially.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian "Завжди я хочу" allows adverb-first word order

errors.original: I want come home and play computer games.
errors.corrected: I want to come home and play computer games.
errors.explanation: Missing "to" in "want to + infinitive" construction.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: - Ukrainian "хочу прийти" does not require a preposition between "want" and the infinitive
